summary: Jess Frazelle revisits her earlier critique of Intel SGX after learning it was originally designed for DRM, not as a general execution environment. She acknowledges the HAVEN paper's novelty in repurposing enclaves for code execution and praises Joanna Rutkowska's thoughtful approach to SGX tradeoffs at Golem. The post emphasizes the importance of understanding a technology's original design intent before judging its limitations, and reflects on how users inevitably push technologies beyond their intended purposes.
key_points: SGX was originally built for DRM use cases, not general code execution
The HAVEN paper was truly novel in proposing enclaves as execution environments
Understanding original design intent makes apparent limitations more forgivable
Users will always push technologies beyond their intended purposes
Computing is fundamentally about understanding and accepting tradeoffs
money_quotes: Having this context makes the problems that arise when you try to do code execution inside an enclave seem like a forgivable sin.
The idea of executing code in an environment with encrypted memory as a way to use the cloud without trusting the cloud is a respectable feat.
I learned from experience with everything I've worked on that people will use it for things it was not built for. This happened a lot with Docker.
It's always fun to see the new ways people use what you build and then to iterate considering the new use cases.
key_insight: Technologies should be evaluated in the context of their original design intent, and what appears to be a flaw may simply be users pushing the technology into use cases it was never built to handle.
